title: restore: added --ignore-static-ip option
body: If a container is restored multiple times from an exported checkpoint with the help of '--import --name', the restore will fail if during 'podman run' a static container IP was set with '--ip'. The user can tell the restore process to ignore the static IP with '--ignore-static-ip'.

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *adrianreber*, *mheon* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Flibpod). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/OWNERS)~~ [mheon] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
What about hostname?  Any other fields?

> What about hostname? Any other fields?

IP address is special as it directly effects the container host and as it is not possible to start two containers with the same IP. It is probably doable in theory but CNI does not do it.

On restore I already replace all the fields I was able to find with the new ID of the container. Hostname is special as I do not change it on restore.

In the example I just tested I am starting a container and it gets the ID 1017af7e2f31 which is also the hostname. On a restore without `--name` the hostname and the ID stays the same. If I restore the same container a second time, it gets a new ID: 119b40ae0abc. The hostname, however, stays the same: 1017af7e2f31.

The reason for not changing the hostname is that the process in the container might already have read the hostname via `gethostname()` and stored that value somewhere internally. Therefore the hostname is not changed after restore to provide the same environment for the process before and after checkpoint/restore. Right now I do not see this as a problem.

If there would ever be a DNS resolver which knows the IP address and hostname of each container this will get complicated if the container with the same hostname exists multiple times with different IPs.

I believe that the way Docker's libnetwork handles internal DNS - and how we'd want to do it - is to map container name, not hostname, to IP. As name is guaranteed to be unique (and may not match hostname), this ought to be fine.
Anyways tests are not happy.  @adrianreber We are cutting a release tomorrow, so if you want this in, you got to get it green.
Test failures are probably our Fedora registry flakes. Tagging for 1.5.0 anyways.
